slides.title: Understanding Markov Decision Process (MDP)
slides.subtitle: A Comprehensive Guide to MDP in Reinforcement Learning

slides.title: Introduction to MDP
slides.content: Markov Decision Process (MDP) is a mathematical framework for modeling decision-making where outcomes are partly random and partly under the control of a decision maker. It provides a formalization of sequential decision making.

slides.title: Components of MDP
slides.content: An MDP consists of states, actions, transition model, and a reward function. These components work together to model the environment and the agent's interaction with it.

slides.title: State and Action
slides.content: The state represents the current situation or configuration of the environment. An action is a decision taken by the agent that affects the state of the environment. Transition probabilities determine the likelihood of moving from one state to another after an action.

slides.title: Example: Grid World
slides.content: Consider a grid world where an agent navigates a grid to reach a goal. Each cell represents a state, and actions are movements in the grid. The agent receives a reward for reaching the goal.

slides.title: Policy and Value Function
slides.content: A policy is a strategy that defines the actions to be taken based on the current state. The value function estimates the expected return of a state, helping to evaluate how good a state is under a policy.

slides.title: Bellman Equation
slides.content: The Bellman Equation is fundamental in solving MDPs. It provides a recursive decomposition of the value function, allowing for efficient computation of optimal policies.

slides.title: Conclusion
slides.content: Markov Decision Processes provide a robust framework for modeling decision making in uncertain environments, forming the basis for many reinforcement learning algorithms.
